title: Multi-scale reconstruction of single-ion damage tracks in diamond via nitrogen-vacancy centers
abstract: Understanding particle-induced damage tracks in solid-state materials underpins emerging applications in rare-event detection and quantum defect engineering. Resolving these tracks requires multi-scale readout, from event localization at the millimeter scale to track-morphology reconstruction at the nanoscale. Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond provide such a platform, combining optical localization with quantum sensing of track morphology. Here, we implant sub-MeV carbon ions into nitrogen-rich diamond and detect individual recoil events via spatially localized NV formation. We develop a simulation framework that explains the observed NV yield and predicts that directional information is retained in the NV distribution after annealing. Machine learning further recovers much of the information lost to defect diffusion and limited NV yield, improving head-tail classification to a level comparable to pre-annealed vacancy tracks. Measurements of NV spin coherence indicate compatibility with nanoscale track reconstruction via NV strain mapping and magnetic gradient-based techniques. These results identify promising pathways toward NV-diamond directional detectors for rare events, while the track-modeling framework has broader implications for paleodetection and quantum material synthesis.

bite.hook: Machine learning and diamond quantum defects can reconstruct the collision tracks of sub-MeV carbon ions.
tldr: They investigated multi-scale reconstruction of particle-induced damage tracks in solid-state materials.
They implanted sub-MeV carbon ions into diamond, detected recoil events via NV center formation, and used machine learning to model defect diffusion.
They found that directional information survives annealing, and machine learning successfully recovers track data lost to defect diffusion.
bite.whyItMatters: This technique could enable directional dark matter detectors and advanced paleodetection, allowing scientists to reconstruct the paths of ancient or rare subatomic particles frozen in solid crystals.
bite.eli5: When a fast particle crashes into a diamond, it leaves a microscopic trail of damage like a bowling ball hitting pins. By looking at how the diamond's atoms were displaced and using smart computer algorithms, scientists can figure out exactly which direction the particle came from.

glossary.shortDef: A heat treatment process that alters the physical and chemical properties of a material.
glossary.eli5Def: Heating up a material and cooling it slowly to let its atoms settle into a more stable arrangement.

glossary.shortDef: The lifetime over which a quantum system maintains its fragile quantum state.
glossary.eli5Def: How long a tiny quantum particle can keep spinning in a steady, predictable way before getting disturbed.

glossary.shortDef: The search for historical particle interactions preserved in natural minerals over geological timescales.
glossary.eli5Def: Looking at ancient rocks and crystals to find microscopic scars left behind by dark matter millions of years ago.
